preFix.title: Insulating a timber-frame stud wall in a self-build extension
preFix.subtitle: Mineral wool, membranes, and service cavity for a warm, airtight timber-frame panel
preFix.excerpt: A timber-frame wall built for a self-build extension or garden room achieves a U-value of 0.18-0.22 W/m²·K with 140 mm mineral wool between the studs, a vapour control layer on the warm side, and a 25 mm service cavity. This is the construction sequence and membrane specification for a correctly detailed frame.
preFix.sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Approved Document L1B (2022) for extensions to existing dwellings: U-value target 0.28 W/m²·K for walls, recommended 0.18 W/m²·K with enhanced specification. NHBC Standards Chapter 6.2 for timber-frame construction. BRE Good Building Guide 44 on timber-frame details.

content.text: A timber-frame wall in a self-build extension has several layers working together: external cladding, a breather membrane, structural sheathing, the stud frame filled with insulation, a vapour control layer, a service cavity, and the internal finish. The membrane positions are critical. The breather membrane on the outside keeps wind-driven rain out while letting moisture vapour escape. The vapour control layer on the inside slows moisture from moving inward from the room.

For a typical 4 m x 3 m single-storey extension with three external walls, materials for the wall panels cost roughly 1,800-2,500 pounds. A 140 mm stud at 400 mm centres fully filled with mineral wool gives a U-value of about 0.25 W/m²·K once stud thermal bridging is accounted for. Add a 50 mm layer of continuous insulation over the studs before the internal finish to break the
thermal bridge
path through the studs themselves), and the
U-value
drops to approximately 0.18 W/m²·K.
Construction sequence

Erect the stud frame: 145 mm x 45 mm C16 regularised timber studs at 400 mm centres for standard loads, 600 mm for garden-room loads only. Head plate and sole plate same section. Brace diagonally to prevent racking before sheathing is applied.

Fix structural sheathing: 9 mm OSB-3 (oriented strand board, exterior grade) to the outside face of the studs. Stagger the vertical joints; fasten at 150 mm centres to the studs with 50 mm galvanised annular ring nails. The sheathing provides racking resistance; it also provides a substrate for the breather membrane.

Apply breather membrane to the sheathing: horizontally lapped sheets (lower sheet first, upper overlapping by at least 150 mm), stapled to the sheathing and taped at all laps and penetrations. The breather membrane is the primary weather barrier during construction.

Fix the external cladding: timber board and batten, brick slip, or render on a rainscreen cavity. The cavity (minimum 25 mm, typically 50 mm) behind the cladding allows wind-driven rain to drain clear of the breather membrane.

Install insulation from the inside: 140 mm mineral wool friction-fit batts between the studs. Cut each batt 5-10 mm wider than the stud spacing so it holds in place without slumping. Fill around any blocking, noggings, and cables without compressing the batt.
Apply the
vapour control layer

: 1000-gauge polythene or a dedicated VCL membrane across the inside face of all insulated stud bays. Tape all joints with a proprietary airtightness tape; lap at least 150 mm at sheet joints. Tape to the structural elements at the perimeter (floor joist header, head plate, corners).

Fit the service cavity: 25 mm x 50 mm timber battens horizontal across the studs, screwed through the VCL into the studs at 400 mm centres. Services (cables, pipes) run in this cavity without penetrating the VCL. This is the most important airtightness detail: most VCL failures happen when sockets, switches, and pipes are buried through the membrane without sealing.
Fix 12.5 mm plasterboard to the battens and skim or tape-and-joint as required.
Done well vs done badly

Done well: the VCL is continuous across the entire wall area with all joints taped and the perimeter sealed. The service cavity keeps all electrical work out of the membrane. The insulation is fully friction-filled with no gaps. Airtightness testing shows less than 5 m³/h/m² at 50 Pa for the extension envelope.

Done badly: VCL cut around socket boxes without airtightness gaskets, insulation compressed behind pipes, breather membrane not lapped at the wall-roof junction. The extension is cold and shows condensation at the stud positions within the first winter.

attrs.fix: Install a layer of 50 mm continuous rigid insulation (PIR or wood fibre) across the internal face of the studs before the service cavity. This breaks the bridge path.
attrs.cause: Thermal bridging through the studs; the insulation only fills between the studs, not over them
attrs.symptom: Cold vertical stripes on the internal wall surface corresponding to stud positions

attrs.fix: Confirm the VCL is continuous and all penetrations are sealed. If any part of the VCL is missing, open the plasterboard at that section and reinstate the membrane.
attrs.cause: VCL is incomplete or missing; moisture from the room interior is reaching the cold sheathing
attrs.symptom: Condensation on the sheathing board visible through the breather membrane
